agent_output: {"executive_summary":"For a small courier team (3 engineers) needing reliable, non-latency-critical delivery notifications across expandable channels, Option B (event-driven with a managed queue) is the recommended architecture — but scoped to a lightweight implementation using a managed service like AWS SQS or Google Cloud Tasks, not a full Kafka/RabbitMQ deployment. Option A (synchronous REST) is simpler to start but creates tight coupling, makes retry logic fragile, and turns channel expansion into a compounding maintenance burden. The 5-minute latency tolerance is the key architectural signal: it unlocks asynchronous processing, which aligns perfectly with the reliability and extensibility requirements.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Delivery Service","responsibility":"Owns delivery lifecycle. Emits domain events (DeliveryDispatched, DeliveryInTransit, DeliveryCompleted, DeliveryFailed) when state transitions occur.","boundaries":"Knows nothing about notification channels. Its only contract is publishing a well-defined domain event to the queue. Zero dependency on email/SMS/push providers."},{"name":"Event Queue (Managed)","responsibility":"Durable, at-least-once message delivery. Acts as the buffer and retry backbone. SQS, Cloud Tasks, or BullMQ on Redis.","boundaries":"Infrastructure component. No business logic. Provides dead-letter queue (DLQ) for poison messages."},{"name":"Notification Worker","responsibility":"Consumes domain events, resolves customer notification preferences, renders templates, dispatches to channel-specific providers. Handles retries at the provider level.","boundaries":"Owns the notification domain: channel routing, template rendering, preference lookup. Does NOT own delivery business logic."},{"name":"Channel Adapters","responsibility":"Thin wrappers around external provider APIs (SendGrid, Twilio, etc.). Implement a common NotificationChannel interface.","boundaries":"Each adapter is isolated. Adding WhatsApp means adding one adapter — no changes to worker orchestration logic."},{"name":"Notification Preference Store","responsibility":"Stores per-customer channel preferences and opt-out status.","boundaries":"Simple CRUD. Could be a table in the existing database initially."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Delivery Service","to":"Event Queue","contract":"Publishes DeliveryStatusChanged event: {deliveryId, customerId, status, timestamp, metadata}","coupling_type":"loose — fire-and-forget via queue"},{"from":"Event Queue","to":"Notification Worker","contract":"Worker polls/receives events. At-least-once delivery semantics. Messages include a deduplication ID.","coupling_type":"loose — queue-mediated"},{"from":"Notification Worker","to":"Channel Adapters","contract":"NotificationChannel interface: send(recipient, templateId, context): Result<Success, RetryableError | PermanentError>","coupling_type":"loose — polymorphic dispatch via interface"},{"from":"Channel Adapters","to":"External Providers","contract":"HTTP REST calls to SendGrid, Twilio, etc.","coupling_type":"tight to provider API — isolated behind adapter boundary"}],"data_flow":"Delivery state change → Delivery Service publishes DeliveryStatusChanged event to queue → Notification Worker consumes event → looks up customer preferences → selects channels → renders templates → dispatches via Channel Adapters → on failure: message returns to queue with backoff (up to N retries) → after max retries: message lands in DLQ for manual inspection.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Event-Driven Architecture (Option B)","rationale":"The 5-minute latency tolerance makes async viable. Decouples the delivery domain from notification concerns entirely. The queue provides durability — failed notifications are retried by infrastructure, not application code."},{"pattern":"Channel Adapter / Strategy Pattern","rationale":"Each notification channel (email, SMS, push, WhatsApp) implements a common interface. Adding a channel is additive — no modification to existing code. Directly addresses the extensibility requirement."},{"pattern":"Dead Letter Queue","rationale":"Messages that fail after max retries land in a DLQ for human inspection. Directly addresses the 'never silently drop' reliability requirement without complex orchestration."},{"pattern":"Idempotent Consumer","rationale":"At-least-once delivery means duplicates are possible. Workers use the event's deduplication ID to prevent sending duplicate notifications. Critical for SMS where duplicates cost money and annoy customers."},{"pattern":"Transactional Outbox (future consideration)","rationale":"If dual-write concerns arise (DB commit succeeds but queue publish fails), the outbox pattern ensures consistency. Not needed day one if using a simple polling approach, but a known evolution path."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Delivery","classification":"core","aggregates":["Delivery (root: deliveryId, states: dispatched/in-transit/completed/failed, emits DeliveryStatusChanged events)"]},{"name":"Notification","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["NotificationRequest (root: notificationId, tracks: channels attempted, delivery status per channel, retry count)","CustomerPreference (root: customerId, tracks: preferred channels, opt-out flags)"]},{"name":"Channel Integration","classification":"generic","aggregates":["Thin adapters — no domain aggregates. Stateless wrappers around external APIs."]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Delivery","downstream":"Notification","relationship":"Customer-Supplier. Delivery defines the event schema. Notification conforms to it. If the Delivery team changes events, Notification must adapt — but the queue provides temporal decoupling."},{"upstream":"Notification","downstream":"Channel Integration","relationship":"Open Host Service. Notification defines the NotificationChannel interface. Channel adapters conform to it."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Option B (Event-Driven) over Option A (Synchronous REST)","gains":"Reliability via queue-based retry without custom retry logic in the delivery service. Clean separation of concerns — delivery service stays focused on delivery. Channel extensibility is additive. Failed provider doesn't block or slow delivery operations. Natural backpressure handling.","sacrifices":"More moving parts than direct REST calls. Requires operating a queue (mitigated by using a managed service). Slightly harder to debug end-to-end — need correlation IDs and logging. Eventual consistency: notification is not confirmed sent when the delivery API returns.","alternatives_considered":["Option A: Synchronous REST. Simpler initially but retry logic must live in the delivery service, creating coupling. Each new channel adds another synchronous call, increasing latency and failure surface. A single provider outage can cascade into delivery service timeouts. Rejected because it optimizes for day-one simplicity at the cost of week-20 pain.","Hybrid: Synchronous for email (most reliable), async for SMS. Rejected — unnecessary complexity, inconsistent patterns, confuses the team."]},{"decision":"Managed queue (SQS/Cloud Tasks) over self-hosted (RabbitMQ/Kafka)","gains":"Zero operational burden for the 3-person team. Built-in DLQ, retry policies, and monitoring. Scales without intervention.","sacrifices":"Vendor coupling. Less control over routing topology. Cost at very high volume (unlikely for a courier company).","alternatives_considered":["RabbitMQ — powerful but requires operational expertise the team doesn't have.","Kafka — massive overkill for notification dispatch. Designed for event streaming, not task queues.","BullMQ on Redis — viable middle ground if already running Redis. Simpler than RabbitMQ, more control than SQS."]},{"decision":"Single Notification Worker service (not per-channel workers)","gains":"One deployable, one codebase, one monitoring dashboard. Appropriate for 3-engineer team. Channel routing is just a function call within the worker.","sacrifices":"Cannot independently scale channels. A spike in SMS doesn't scale independently from email processing.","alternatives_considered":["Per-channel workers with separate queues. More scalable but premature — a courier company's notification volume doesn't justify it. Can split later if needed."]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Channel Adapters","evolution_path":"Adding push notifications or WhatsApp means implementing one new class conforming to NotificationChannel interface, registering it in the channel router, and adding it to customer preference options. Zero changes to the Delivery Service, queue infrastructure, or worker orchestration."},{"area":"Notification Templates","evolution_path":"Templates can start as simple string interpolation, evolve to a template engine (Handlebars, Mustache), then to a template management UI — all within the Notification bounded context."},{"area":"Queue Infrastructure","evolution_path":"Start with SQS/Cloud Tasks. If the team outgrows it or needs complex routing, migrate to RabbitMQ. The Delivery Service only knows it publishes events — the queue is an infrastructure detail."},{"area":"Per-Channel Scaling","evolution_path":"If one channel becomes a bottleneck, split into per-channel queues and workers. The NotificationChannel interface means the worker can be decomposed without changing adapters."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"DeliveryStatusChanged Event Schema","reason":"This is the contract between Delivery and Notification contexts. Changes here ripple downstream. Must be versioned from day one. Use a schema definition (even a TypeScript type or JSON Schema) committed to the repo."},{"area":"At-Least-Once Delivery Semantics","reason":"The entire retry and reliability model depends on this queue guarantee. Switching to at-most-once would break the reliability requirement. This is a deliberate, load-bearing constraint."},{"area":"Idempotency Keys","reason":"Once the deduplication strategy is chosen (event ID-based), changing it risks duplicate or lost notifications. Design this carefully upfront."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Dual-write problem: delivery state saved to DB but event fails to publish to queue, resulting in a status change with no notification","severity":"high","mitigation":"Phase 1: accept the small risk — queue publish failures are rare with managed services and can be caught by monitoring. Phase 2: implement transactional outbox pattern if the failure rate is non-trivial. Do NOT over-engineer this on day one."},{"risk":"Dead letter queue fills up without anyone noticing — notifications silently lost in practice","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Set up DLQ alerting from day one. A CloudWatch/PagerDuty alarm on DLQ depth > 0 is trivial and non-negotiable. This is the most important operational control in the system."},{"risk":"Provider API changes break channel adapters","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Channel adapters are isolated — a breaking change in Twilio's API only affects the SMS adapter. Pin provider SDK versions and monitor deprecation notices."},{"risk":"Team lacks async/event-driven experience and introduces subtle bugs (lost messages, duplicate processing)","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use a managed queue with built-in retry policies — this removes most footguns. Write integration tests that simulate provider failures and verify retry behavior. Keep the architecture as simple as possible within the event-driven pattern."},{"risk":"Notification preferences not checked, leading to messages sent to opted-out customers (compliance risk)","severity":"high","mitigation":"Preference check is a mandatory step in the worker pipeline, not an optional filter. Enforce at the code level with a guard clause that rejects dispatch if preferences aren't loaded."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Start with Option B using a managed queue (SQS if on AWS, Cloud Tasks if on GCP, BullMQ if self-hosting with Redis already available). Build a single Notification Worker service with a plugin-style channel adapter registry. Ship email + SMS adapters first. The Delivery Service publishes DeliveryStatusChanged events and forgets about them. Implement idempotency using the event ID. Set up DLQ alerting before going live — this is a launch blocker, not a nice-to-have.","files_to_create_or_modify":["src/delivery/events/DeliveryStatusChanged.ts — Domain event definition with schema","src/delivery/services/DeliveryService.ts — Publish event on state transitions","src/notification/worker/NotificationWorker.ts — Queue consumer, orchestrates channel dispatch","src/notification/channels/NotificationChannel.ts — Interface definition","src/notification/channels/EmailAdapter.ts — SendGrid/SES adapter","src/notification/channels/SmsAdapter.ts — Twilio adapter","src/notification/preferences/PreferenceService.ts — Customer channel preference lookup","src/notification/templates/TemplateRenderer.ts — Message template rendering","src/infrastructure/queue/QueuePublisher.ts — Abstraction over queue client","src/infrastructure/queue/QueueConsumer.ts — Abstraction over queue polling","infrastructure/terraform/sqs.tf (or equivalent) — Queue + DLQ + alerting"],"patterns_to_follow":"Strategy pattern for channel adapters. Interface segregation — NotificationChannel has only send(). Retry via queue visibility timeout / redelivery, NOT application-level retry loops. Structured logging with correlation IDs (deliveryId, notificationId) on every log line. Feature flags for new channels during rollout.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT build retry logic inside the Delivery Service — that's the queue's job","Do NOT use a shared database between Delivery and Notification contexts — communicate via events only","Do NOT skip idempotency — at-least-once means duplicates WILL happen","Do NOT deploy without DLQ alerting — this single control prevents the 'silently dropped' failure mode","Do NOT abstract the queue behind a generic event bus on day one — YAGNI. Direct queue client abstraction is sufficient","Do NOT use fan-out to multiple queues per channel yet — one queue, one worker, channel routing in code. Split when volume demands it"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":88,"high_confidence_areas":["Event-driven is the right pattern given the latency tolerance and reliability requirement","Managed queue is the right infrastructure choice for a 3-person team","Channel adapter pattern is the correct extensibility mechanism","DLQ alerting is a non-negotiable operational control"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific queue technology choice depends on the team's existing cloud provider and infrastructure, which is unknown","Whether BullMQ on Redis is viable depends on existing Redis availability","Transactional outbox necessity depends on actual dual-write failure rates in production"],"assumptions":["Team is deploying to a major cloud provider (AWS, GCP, or Azure) with managed queue services available","Notification volume is moderate (thousands per day, not millions) — typical for a courier company","Team has basic experience with async patterns or is willing to learn","Existing delivery service can be modified to emit events"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"For a small courier team (3 engineers) needing reliable, non-latency-critical delivery notifications across expandable channels, Option B (event-driven with a managed queue) is the recommended architecture — but scoped to a lightweight implementation using a managed service like AWS SQS or Google Cloud Tasks, not a full Kafka/RabbitMQ deployment.
